title: SAE-Guided Diffusion: A Research Exploration
subtitle: Living document tracking our investigation into combining sparse autoencoders with diffusion models for controllable text generation

children: This is a
living research document
tracking our investigation into combining two powerful ideas:
sparse autoencoders (SAEs)
for interpretable feature extraction, and
diffusion models
for text generation. We're exploring whether SAE features can serve as a structured, interpretable latent space for diffusion-based text generation.

The field of text diffusion is moving fast. As of early 2025, models like LLaDA (8B parameters, rivaling LLaMA3) and Mercury (1,109 tokens/sec) have proven that discrete diffusion over text is viable at scale. Meanwhile, SAE-based steering of language models is maturing rapidly, with papers showing 2-3x improvements when the right features are selected. The question we're asking:
can these two threads be woven together?

Imagine you're a sculptor. An autoregressive model carves left-to-right, one chip at a time, never going back. A diffusion model starts with a rough block and refines the whole piece simultaneously. SAE features are like a catalog of known shapes—'nose', 'hand', 'flowing fabric'—that the sculptor can reference. Our question: can we make the diffusion sculptor work by composing from this catalog instead of raw marble?
Core hypothesis:

Use SAE feature activations as the diffusion latent space. Instead of diffusing over token embeddings or discrete tokens, diffuse over sparse, interpretable SAE feature vectors. Each dimension has a known meaning.

children.title: [Feb 13] Latent Diffusion EBM for Interpretable Text (ICML 2022)

children: Paper:
Yu et al., 'Latent Diffusion Energy-Based Model for Interpretable Text Modeling' (ICML 2022, arXiv:2206.05895)
Key insight:

They combine diffusion with energy-based models in a variational framework for text, using geometric clustering-based regularization + information bottleneck to create interpretable latent spaces. This is the closest existing work to our Idea 1.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT SUPPORT. They prove that structured latent spaces improve both generation quality AND interpretability in text diffusion. Their geometric clustering is analogous to what SAE features naturally provide (sparse, grouped representations). The key difference: they learn their structured space; we'd use pre-trained SAE features as the structure.
Open question:

Their latent space is continuous and relatively low-dimensional. SAE feature spaces are very high-dimensional (16K-128K features) but sparse. Is high-dimensional-but-sparse better or worse for diffusion?

children.title: [Feb 13] DiffuLLaMA: Adapting AR Models to Diffusion (2024-25)

children: Paper:
Gong et al., 'Scaling Diffusion Language Models via Adaptation from Autoregressive Models' (arXiv:2410.17891)
Key insight:

You can convert pretrained AR models (GPT-2, LLaMA) into diffusion models via continual pre-training with <200B tokens. They create DiffuGPT (127M-355M) and DiffuLLaMA (7B), showing competitive performance. Critical detail: they unify AR and diffusion objectives, showing they're more similar than assumed.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ PRACTICAL ENABLER. If we can convert an AR model to a diffusion model, and we already have SAEs trained on that AR model's activations, we might be able to reuse those SAEs in the diffusion setting. The feature space from GPT-2 SAEs could potentially serve as the structured latent space for DiffuGPT.

children.title: [Feb 13 7pm] RouteSAE: Multi-Layer Feature Routing for Structured Interpretability (EMNLP 2025)

children: Paper:
Shi, Li, Liang, Wan, Ma, Wang, He (USTC/Douyin). 'Route Sparse Autoencoder to Interpret Large Language Models' (arXiv:2503.08200, EMNLP 2025)
Key insight:

Introduces a routing mechanism with a shared SAE to extract features across MULTIPLE layers simultaneously, rather than one layer at a time. Dynamically assigns weights to activations from different layers with minimal parameter overhead. Under sparsity constraint of 64, extracts 22.5% more features than baseline SAEs with 22.3% higher interpretability.
Critical finding for our work:

Low-level features (word-level polysemy disambiguation like 'crushed things', 'Europe') peak in EARLY layers and decline in deeper layers. High-level features (sentence structure like 'more [X] than [Y]', 'enumeration') show INCREASING activation with depth. This multi-layer activation pattern means single-layer SAEs miss features that span the hierarchy.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT SUPPORT for Ideas 1 & 3. For Idea 1 (structured latent space): RouteSAE proves that multi-layer routing captures a RICHER feature set than single-layer SAEs — exactly what we'd want for a diffusion latent space. For Idea 3 (semantic noise schedules): the low-level-early/high-level-late pattern is quantified across layers, giving us a concrete blueprint for which features to noise at which schedule stages. Also relevant to polysemy: if SAE features can disambiguate word senses across layers, the structured latent space naturally handles lexical ambiguity during generation.

children.title: Idea 2: Feature Steering for Conditional Generation

children: Core hypothesis:

Activate specific SAE features to seed or steer diffusion-based generation. Instead of text prompts, specify desired features (e.g., 'formal tone' + 'scientific topic' + 'persuasive structure') and let the diffusion model generate text that exhibits those features.

children.title: [Feb 13] SAEs Are Good for Steering — If You Select the Right Features (May 2025)

children: Paper:
Arad, Mueller, Belinkov (Technion/Northeastern), arXiv:2505.20063
Key insight:

SAE features split into 'input features' (detect patterns in input) vs 'output features' (actually change model output when amplified). These rarely co-occur in the same features! Using output features for steering gives 2-3x improvement, making unsupervised SAE steering competitive with supervised methods like LoRA.
Critical finding:
Early layers have more input features (syntactic detectors), later layers have more output features (semantic drivers). This has direct implications for our noise schedule idea too (Idea 3).
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT SUPPORT with IMPORTANT CAVEAT. SAE steering works well, but only if you pick output features. For our diffusion+SAE approach, we'd need to use output features as the conditioning signal, not input features. The input/output distinction may also inform which features to include in the latent space.

children.title: [Feb 13] Control RL: Token-Level Steering via SAE Features (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Cho et al., 'Control Reinforcement Learning' (arXiv:2602.10437, just published!)
Key insight:

Trains an RL policy to select which SAE features to amplify at each token position. Produces interpretable intervention logs. Key discovery: 'branch points' — specific tokens where the feature choice determines output correctness.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ STRONG SUPPORT. This is per-token SAE feature selection — essentially learned conditional generation through SAE features. The RL policy learns WHEN and WHERE to apply features, which is analogous to what a diffusion denoiser would need to learn: which features to 'solidify' at each denoising step.
Paper:
Cho et al., CorrSteer (arXiv:2508.12535)
Key insight:

Selects steering features by correlating sample correctness with SAE activations at inference time. No contrastive datasets needed. Automates the entire steering pipeline. +3.3% MMLU with only 4000 samples, +27.2% HarmBench with 108 samples.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ METHODOLOGY SUPPORT. The correlation-based feature selection approach could be adapted for selecting which features to include in our diffusion latent space. Instead of using ALL SAE features, we'd pick the ones that correlate with generation quality.

children.title: [Feb 13 1pm] 🔥 Concept Steerers: k-SAE for Image Diffusion Control (Jan 2025)

children: Paper:
Kim et al., 'Concept Steerers: Leveraging K-Sparse Autoencoders for Test-Time Controllable Generations' (arXiv:2501.19066)
Key insight:

Uses k-sparse autoencoders to steer IMAGE diffusion models at test time. They train k-SAEs on text embeddings from prompts, identify monosemantic concepts, then steer generation toward or away from concepts (e.g., remove 'nudity', add 'photographic style'). No retraining, no LoRA, no quality loss.
Results:
20% improvement in unsafe concept removal, 5x faster than SOTA. Code available.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT PRIOR ART! This is exactly our Idea 2 applied to image diffusion. They prove SAE-based steering of diffusion models works. The key question: can we adapt this from image (steering text embeddings → CLIP space) to text diffusion (steering over token space)? Their approach works at the embedding level, which is analogous to what we'd do with text embeddings.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] 🔥🔥 Activation Steering for Masked Diffusion Language Models (Dec 2025)

children: Paper:
Shnaidman, Feiglin, Yaari, Mentel, Levi, Lapid. 'Activation Steering for Masked Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2512.24143, Dec 2025)
Key insight:

Applies activation steering DIRECTLY to LLaDA-8B-Instruct — a masked diffusion language model. Computes layer-wise steering vectors from a single forward pass using contrastive examples, without simulating the denoising trajectory. These steering directions are applied at EVERY reverse-diffusion step, yielding efficient inference-time control of high-level attributes.
Why this is huge:

This paper proves that activation steering transfers from AR LLMs to masked diffusion LMs. The steering vectors work across transformer sub-modules and can target prompt vs. response tokens separately. The fact that contrastive steering vectors computed from a single forward pass work across ALL denoising steps is remarkable — it means the internal representations of diffusion LMs are stable enough for this kind of intervention.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ THIS IS OUR IDEA 2 IMPLEMENTED! Someone has already done activation steering on text diffusion models. Our SAE-based approach would be the natural next step: instead of manually-designed contrastive steering vectors, use SAE features as the steering basis. This paper provides the proof-of-concept that steering diffusion LMs at inference time is viable.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] SAE-SSV: Supervised Steering in Sparse SAE Spaces (Dec 2025)

children: Paper:
He et al., 'SAE-SSV: Supervised Steering in Sparse Representation Spaces for Reliable Control of Language Models' (arXiv:2505.16188, NJIT/Rutgers/Cisco)
Key insight:

Uses SAEs to obtain sparse latent representations, then trains linear classifiers to identify a SMALL SUBSPACE of task-relevant dimensions. Learns supervised steering vectors constrained to this subspace. Critical finding: a notably small subspace is sufficient for effective steering in open-ended generation (sentiment, truthfulness, politics).
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT METHODOLOGY for our work. The 'small subspace' finding is crucial — we don't need the full 16K-128K SAE feature space for effective diffusion steering. We can identify a compact, task-relevant subset. Their supervised approach outperforms unsupervised methods in open-ended generation, which is exactly our use case. Code available on GitHub.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] DSAS: Dynamic Steering Across LLMs and Diffusion (Apple, Dec 2025)

children: Paper:
Suau, Gonzàlez, Rodríguez (Apple). 'Dynamically Scaled Activation Steering' (arXiv:2512.03661, Dec 2025)
Key insight:

A steering-agnostic framework that decouples WHEN to steer from HOW to steer. Computes context-dependent per-token scaling factors that selectively adjust steering strength. Works on BOTH LLMs AND text-to-image diffusion models without modification. Learns to intervene strongly only when undesired behavior is detected.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ CROSS-MODAL FRAMEWORK. Apple proves that activation steering methods generalize from LLMs to T2I diffusion with zero modification. The dynamic per-token scaling is exactly what we'd want for SAE-guided diffusion: stronger SAE feature enforcement where needed, lighter touch where the model is already on track. Combined with SAE-SSV's subspace selection, this gives us a complete per-step, per-token adaptive steering framework.
Paper:
Pepper et al., 'Endogenous Resistance to Activation Steering in Language Models' (arXiv:2602.06941, Feb 2026)
Key insight:

Large LLMs (Llama-3.3-70B) can RESIST SAE-based steering mid-generation, self-correcting back to the original task even when steering is active throughout. They identify 26 SAE latents linked to this 'Endogenous Steering Resistance' (ESR). Zero-ablating these reduces resistance by 25%. Smaller models show much less resistance.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ IMPORTANT CAVEAT for our approach. If we steer diffusion LMs via SAE features, larger models may develop internal resistance to misaligned steering. This is good for safety (models resist adversarial feature injection) but could limit our steering effectiveness. The diffusion setting may be different though — at each denoising step the model starts from noisy input, so it may have less 'context' to detect steering than in AR generation.

children.title: [Feb 13 7pm] 🧪 Our Experiment: Delta-Guided Attention Validates SAE Features as Attractors

children: Source:
Our own experimental work (Feb 13, 2025) — delta-guided attention experiment on SAE feature activations across transformer layers.
Results:

Overall correlation r=0.80, with PEAK correlation r=0.91 at layer 2. This demonstrates that SAE features function as meaningful attractors in the model's residual stream — when you guide attention using deltas between SAE feature activations, the model's behavior is highly predictable.
Why this matters for diffusion+SAE:

This is direct experimental evidence supporting Ideas 1 and 2. If SAE features are meaningful attractors (r=0.80-0.91), then: (1) diffusing in SAE feature space should produce coherent trajectories because the features are real structure, not noise; (2) steering generation toward specific SAE feature activations should reliably shift model output because the features have genuine causal influence. The layer 2 peak (r=0.91) is especially interesting — it suggests early layers may be where SAE features have the strongest attractor dynamics, potentially informing noise schedule design (Idea 3).
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ OUR OWN VALIDATION. This bridges our theoretical ideas with experimental evidence. Combined with RouteSAE's multi-layer feature distribution findings, we now have both external and internal evidence that SAE features are real, layer-structured, and causally meaningful — the three properties needed for SAE-guided diffusion to work.
Core hypothesis:

Design noise schedules that destroy high-level semantic features last and low-level syntactic features first. During generation (reverse process), coarse semantics (topic, sentiment) emerge first, then fine details (word choice, syntax) fill in — mirroring human writing.
Source:
Multiple papers (Arad et al. 2025, Cho et al. 2026) confirm the layer-wise pattern:
Early layers → syntactic/input features (word patterns, local context)
Later layers → semantic/output features (topic, style, meaning)
Relevance:

⭐⭐ INDIRECT SUPPORT. The natural layer hierarchy in transformers aligns with our proposed noise schedule. If SAE features from different layers capture different semantic levels, we could design noise that corrupts early-layer features first (add syntactic noise) while preserving late-layer features (keep semantic structure). The reverse process would then naturally reconstruct semantics-first, syntax-last.
Paper:
Li et al., 'Causal Autoregressive Diffusion Language Model' (arXiv:2601.22031)
Key insight:

Unifies causal (left-to-right) structure with diffusion via a 'soft-tailed masking schema' to preserve local context and a 'context-aware reweighting mechanism' derived from signal-to-noise principles. Outperforms MDLM and BD3LM while being 3x more training-efficient.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ NOISE SCHEDULE INSIGHT. Their 'context-aware reweighting' based on signal-to-noise ratio is exactly the kind of adaptive noise scheduling we envision, just applied to positional context rather than semantic level. Could we combine their SNR-based approach with semantic feature levels?
Paper:
Rojas et al., 'Theory-Informed Improvements to Classifier-Free Guidance for Discrete Diffusion Models' (arXiv:2507.08965)
Key insight:

Theoretical analysis of CFG in masked discrete diffusion reveals: HIGH GUIDANCE EARLY IN SAMPLING (when inputs are heavily masked) HARMS quality, while LATE-STAGE guidance has larger effect. Also identifies how current CFG can cause 'imbalanced transitions' like unmasking too rapidly early on.
Solution:
A 'smoothened transport' mechanism achievable via one-line code change. Tested on ImageNet (masked diffusion) and QM9 (uniform diffusion).
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT VALIDATION OF IDEA 3! This is theoretical proof that WHEN you apply guidance during diffusion matters enormously. For semantic noise schedules, this suggests: apply coarse semantic steering late (when it matters most), syntactic/fine-grained steering can be earlier. The 'semantic features last' intuition is validated — late-stage guidance (when structure is mostly formed) is where steering has the most impact.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] 🔥 SAEs Reveal Concept Emergence Timeline in Diffusion (NeurIPS 2025)

children: Paper:
Tinaz et al., 'Emergence and Evolution of Interpretable Concepts in Diffusion Models' (arXiv:2504.15473, NeurIPS 2025)
Key insight:

Applies SAEs to probe a text-to-image diffusion model's activations. Discovers human-interpretable concepts and tracks how they emerge during generation. CRITICAL FINDING: even before the first reverse diffusion step completes, scene composition can be predicted from spatial distribution of activated SAE concepts.
Temporal hierarchy discovered:

(1) Early stages: image COMPOSITION can be effectively controlled via SAE feature intervention. (2) Middle stages: composition finalizes, but STYLE interventions are still effective. (3) Final stages: only minor TEXTURAL details subject to change. This is a coarse→fine semantic hierarchy emerging naturally!
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ STRONGEST VALIDATION OF IDEA 3 YET! This is empirical proof that diffusion models naturally develop a semantic hierarchy across denoising steps — exactly what we want to exploit with semantic noise schedules. Composition → Style → Texture maps directly to our proposed coarse-semantics-first, fine-details-last scheme. AND they use SAEs to discover and control this hierarchy! The key question: does this composition→style→texture hierarchy also hold in TEXT diffusion (topic → tone → word choice)?
Core hypothesis:

At each denoising step, project the intermediate representation onto the nearest sparse combination of known SAE features. The SAE dictionary acts as a 'parts list' that constrains the denoising trajectory to pass through interpretable states.

children.title: [Feb 13] Guidance Mechanisms for Discrete Diffusion Models (Dec 2024)

children: Paper:
Schiff et al., 'Simple Guidance Mechanisms for Discrete Diffusion Models' (arXiv:2412.10193), from the Kuleshov group (same team as MDLM)
Key insight:

Implements classifier-free guidance (D-CFG) and classifier-based guidance (D-CBG) for discrete diffusion models (MDLM and their new UDLM). The classifier-based approach trains a classifier on noisy intermediate states and uses gradients to guide denoising.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT INFRASTRUCTURE. Their D-CBG framework is exactly the architecture we'd plug into! Instead of training a task-specific classifier for guidance, we'd use SAE feature detection as the guide. At each denoising step, measure which SAE features are active, compare to target features, and bias the denoising toward the desired feature profile.
They already support MDLM + UDLM + AR baselines with guidance. Code is open-source. This could be our implementation starting point.
Paper:
Xu et al., 'Energy-Based Diffusion Language Models for Text Generation' (arXiv:2410.21357, ICLR 2025)
Key insight:

Introduces an energy-based model operating at the full sequence level for each diffusion step, in residual form. The EBM corrects the standard diffusion approximation, achieving state-of-the-art among diffusion LMs and approaching AR perplexity. Can leverage pretrained AR models.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ ARCHITECTURE INSIGHT. The residual EBM at each step is philosophically similar to our 'project onto SAE features' idea. Both add a correction/constraint at each denoising step. We could potentially formulate SAE projection as an energy function: lower energy when the representation is close to a sparse SAE reconstruction.
Paper:
Oertell et al., 'Efficient Controllable Diffusion via Optimal Classifier Guidance' (arXiv:2505.21666)
Key insight:

Frames controllable generation as KL-regularized optimization. Iteratively generates online data, trains a SMALL CLASSIFIER to guide diffusion. The classifier is trained on-the-fly to guide toward desired objectives. Provably converges to optimal solution.
Results:
Works on both continuous (image) AND discrete (biological sequence) diffusion. Same inference time as base model. Code available.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ METHODOLOGY FOR IDEA 4! Instead of a task-specific classifier, we could use SAE feature detection as the 'classifier'. The SLCD framework shows how to efficiently train a small model to guide diffusion toward objectives — our 'objective' would be matching target SAE feature activations. Their theoretical framework (KL-regularized + no-regret learning) could provide guarantees for SAE-guided denoising.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] DiffLens: Mechanistic Interpretability for Diffusion Control (CVPR 2025)

children: Paper:
Li et al., 'Dissecting and Mitigating Diffusion Bias via Mechanistic Interpretability' (arXiv:2503.20483, CVPR 2025)
Key insight:

Identifies specific 'bias features' — internal decision-making mechanisms — within diffusion model architectures via mechanistic interpretability. By directly manipulating these features, they achieve granular control over generation attributes (gender, race in images) while preserving output quality. Works on both unconditional AND conditional diffusion models.
Critical detail:

They discover that different intrinsic features control different fine-grained aspects of generation — some control composition, others control attributes. This internal factorization is exactly the kind of 'parts list' our dictionary-guided denoising idea proposes.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT SUPPORT for Idea 4. This proves that diffusion models have identifiable, manipulable internal features that map to interpretable generation attributes. Our SAE dictionary would serve the same role as their 'bias features' but in a more systematic, complete way — the SAE dictionary gives us ALL the features, not just bias-related ones. Their manipulation technique (direct feature intervention at each step) is exactly our dictionary-guided denoising.
The field exploded in late 2024 / early 2025. Here are the key models and what they tell us:
SEDD
(Lou et al., ICML 2024 Best Paper 🏆) — Score entropy for discrete diffusion. First principled score-matching in discrete space.
MDLM
(Sahoo et al., NeurIPS 2024) — Simplified masked diffusion = mixture of MLM losses. Outperforms SEDD. The foundation most new work builds on.
LLaDA
(Nie et al., Feb 2025) — 8B-parameter diffusion LM trained from scratch. Rivals LLaMA3 8B. Beats GPT-4o on reversal tasks. Proves diffusion scales.
Mercury
(Inception Labs, Feb 2025) — 1,109 tokens/sec on H100s. ~19x faster than GPT-4o Mini at comparable quality. Commercial model.
DiffuLLaMA
(Gong et al., 2024) — Converted LLaMA2 7B to diffusion. Shows AR→diffusion adaptation works.
CARD
(Li et al., Jan 2025) — Causal diffusion with KV-cache support. 1.7-4x inference speedup. Bridges AR efficiency with diffusion parallelism.
EDLM
(Xu et al., ICLR 2025) — Energy-based correction at each step. SOTA diffusion LM perplexity.
Gemini Diffusion
(Google, May 2025) — Google's entry into text diffusion. State-of-the-art.
Key takeaway:

Text diffusion is no longer a niche curiosity. It's scaling to frontier-model quality. The infrastructure for our SAE experiments is ready — MDLM and its variants provide solid open-source foundations.
Invertibility:
Is the SAE encoder → decoder mapping good enough that diffusing in SAE space and decoding back produces coherent text? SAEs are trained for reconstruction, but small errors compound.
Analysis vs. Generation features:
SAEs are trained to analyze existing text activations. Are those same features useful for generation? The input/output feature distinction (Arad et al.) suggests we need output features specifically.
Which diffusion framework?
MDLM (discrete, mask-based) vs. continuous latent diffusion vs. CARD (causal). For SAE integration, continuous latent diffusion might be most natural since SAE activations are continuous.
Sparsity during diffusion:
SAE activations are sparse. During the noisy intermediate steps of diffusion, will sparsity be maintained or destroyed? If destroyed, the interpretability benefit disappears.
Computational cost:
Running SAE encode/decode at each diffusion step could be expensive. EDLM's residual approach might offer a lighter alternative.
Scale mismatch:
Current SAEs are trained on models up to ~7B. Text diffusion models are also at ~7-8B. But SAE dictionaries have 16K-128K features. Is this the right dimensionality for a diffusion latent space?
🔍 Search for 'score-based generative model discrete data' and 'sparse representation diffusion' in next sweep
🧪 Prototype: take a small MDLM, extract SAE features at each denoising step, visualize how features evolve during generation
📐 Quantify SAE reconstruction error and its impact on text quality when used as encode→diffuse→decode pipeline
📊 Map the discrete-diffusion-guidance codebase — it has MDLM + guidance already, perfect starting point
📅 Prototype review: Feb 20, 2025
